chunks.text: Ludwig van Beethoven, starting in his late twenties, slowly began to lose his hearing.
For a musician, no suffering could have been greater.
Yet silence did not stop the music inside him.
Deafness drove him toward despair, yet that very despair changed his music into something deeper.
In 1824, his final symphony, the Ninth, received its first performance.
It was a grand composition, using a choir to sing Schiller's poem
On the night of the first performance, Beethoven stood on the stage.
He faced the podium, but he could no longer hear any sound.
After the final note, applause filled the hall, yet he stood with his back turned
One of the singer gently touched his arm and made him turn around.
Before him, the audience was on its feet, applauding.
Silence did not lock him inside.
The Ninth is the crystal form of suffering that Beethoven had overcome.
